item.description: A tragic incident occurred in Santa Monica on Friday afternoon when a woman was killed after crawling underneath a stopped Metro bus. According to the Santa Monica Police Department, the fatal collision happened around 2 p.m. at the intersection of Main Street and Ocean Park Boulevard. Preliminary investigations suggest that the woman, believed to be homeless, moved under the bus while it was stopped, and she suffered fatal injuries as the vehicle began moving. Security footage from a nearby business confirmed that the woman crawled under the bus prior to the accident. Authorities stated that there is no indication that the bus driver was impaired or distracted at the time. The driver remained at the scene and cooperated with investigators. Traffic on Main Street was disrupted for several hours between Ocean Park Boulevard and Hollister Avenue while emergency services and investigators responded to the scene. The incident is under investigation by the Santa Monica Police and Fire Depar

item.title: Senate Rules Prevent Trump’s White House Ballroom Funding in Immigration Bill
item.description: President Donald Trump's proposal for a new White House ballroom has encountered a significant procedural obstacle in the Senate. The Senate parliamentarian ruled that $1 billion requested for the ballroom cannot be included in the GOP’s immigration enforcement funding bill under reconciliation rules. This ruling may allow Senate Republicans to avoid a politically challenging vote. Oregon Senator Jeff Merkley, the top Democrat on the Senate Budget Committee, emphasized that Democrats are ready to challenge any attempts to revise the bill to benefit Trump. The parliamentarian determined that the ballroom provision exceeds the jurisdiction of the Senate Judiciary Committee, which initially proposed it. Trump, who previously ordered the demolition of the White House East Wing, originally promised to fund the ballroom through private donations but later sought taxpayer funds specifically for security measures. The project has faced widespread public opposition, and the ruling highlights th

item.title: Bill Cassidy eliminated from Louisiana Senate primary as Letlow and Fleming advance to runoff
item.description: In the 2026 Louisiana Republican Senate primary, incumbent Senator Bill Cassidy failed to secure enough support to reach the runoff, marking a significant political setback. Cassidy, who faced criticism from former President Donald Trump for voting to convict him during his second impeachment related to the January 6, 2021, Capitol attack, received just over 24 percent of the vote. Representative Julia Letlow led the race with over 40 percent, while Louisiana State Treasurer John Fleming claimed the second spot with nearly 30 percent, setting up a runoff between Letlow and Fleming on June 27. Cassidy's elimination reflects the continuing influence of Trump-backed candidates within the GOP and highlights the challenges faced by incumbents who opposed Trump in key votes. The primary outcome is being closely watched as an indicator of Republican voter sentiment heading into the 2026 midterms, particularly in high-profile Senate contests where loyalty to Trump remains a decisive factor. Th

item.title: Napoleon Solo Wins the Preakness Stakes at Laurel Park, Defeating Iron Honor
item.description: Napoleon Solo claimed victory in the Preakness Stakes at Laurel Park, holding off Iron Honor by 1 1/4 lengths. This marked his first win of the year after two fifth-place finishes in earlier races. With Kentucky Derby winner Golden Tempo absent, the race featured a competitive field of 14 horses. Taj Mahal initially led the race but was overtaken by Napoleon Solo near the stretch. Iron Honor challenged late but ultimately finished second. The event had a subdued atmosphere due to attendance being capped at 4,800. Trainer Chad Summers and jockey Paco Lopez celebrated their first Triple Crown race win. Napoleon Solo, who had won the Champagne Stakes last year, paid $17.80 on a $2 bet. Other notable horses, including Ocelli, Incredibolt, and Robusta, finished further back. This Preakness was held at Laurel Park because Pimlico is undergoing reconstruction. The victory positions Napoleon Solo as a horse to watch for the rest of the racing season.

item.title: The Life and Legacy of Norman Hartnell, Royal Fashion Icon
item.description: Sir Norman Hartnell (1901–1979) was a pioneering British fashion designer, renowned as the principal dressmaker for Queen Elizabeth II and the Queen Mother. His career peaked with the creation of two historic garments: the Queen’s wedding dress in 1947 and her Coronation gown in 1953. Hartnell’s designs were characterized by theatrical elegance, detailed embroidery, intricate beadwork, and the use of luxurious fabrics like tulle and satin. Beyond royal commissions, he brought British couture to international prominence, blending classical sophistication with a distinctive English romantic style. Hartnell was the first British designer to be knighted, cementing his legacy in royal tradition and mid-20th-century fashion. The article showcases numerous vintage photographs of Hartnell’s creations, from ball gowns and wedding dresses to stylish coats and tailored suits, highlighting his innovation and timeless appeal. Models such as Della Oake, June Duncan, and Wenda Parkinson wore his crea

item.title: Aaron Rodgers Signs One-Year Contract to Return to Pittsburgh Steelers
item.description: Aaron Rodgers, the four-time NFL MVP, has agreed to a one-year contract to return to the Pittsburgh Steelers, ending a lengthy decision-making period. At 42 years old, Rodgers led the Steelers to an AFC North title last season, throwing 24 touchdowns and seven interceptions. He reunites with former Green Bay Packers coach Mike McCarthy, who became the Steelers’ head coach earlier this year. Rodgers and McCarthy share a 13-year history in Green Bay, where Rodgers earned two of his four MVP awards and a Super Bowl title under McCarthy's guidance. This season is expected to be Rodgers’ 22nd and potentially his final in the NFL, while McCarthy, 64, appears near the end of his coaching career. The Steelers’ management expressed strong interest in bringing Rodgers back, placing an unrestricted free agent tender on him to secure some contractual protection. While the team has added new talent, including receivers Michael Pittman Jr. and Germie Bernard, and drafted quarterback Drew Allar, Rodg

item.title: Former Trump Border Patrol Official Criticizes DHS and Administration Over Immigration Policy
item.description: Greg Bovino, a former high-ranking Border Patrol official during the Trump administration, publicly criticized his former employers and the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) for obstructing stricter immigration enforcement. Speaking with Megyn Kelly, Bovino described other officials as 'swamp creatures' and suggested that political maneuvers, including resurfaced past allegations against Border Patrol Chief Mike Banks, were used to remove hardliners advocating tougher immigration policies. Bovino emphasized that Banks had been cleared of prior allegations and praised him as an effective immigration enforcer. He claimed that the Trump administration had shifted toward a softer approach to immigration following two deaths during enforcement actions in Minneapolis earlier this year. Bovino and other former officials who supported more aggressive deportation policies are now out of DHS, warning that some internal resistance remains. Bovino's comments reflect ongoing internal tensions w

item.title: A Visual Journey Through London Streets in the 1980s
item.description: During the 1980s, London presented a vivid mix of contrasts, where historic Victorian architecture stood alongside the neon glow of a transforming city. The streets became a canvas for emerging cultural movements, with the sounds of post-punk, new wave, and early club music echoing through the neighborhoods. Camden Town showcased rebellious youth with Mohawks and leather jackets, while the financial district maintained its sleek power suits and red buses. Social tensions and creative energy coexisted, giving rise to the New Romantic fashion scene. Photographer Peter Marshall captured these dynamic urban landscapes through a series of photos from 1980 to 1985, documenting everything from industrial sites and markets to pubs, docks, and riverside scenes. Locations include Piccadilly, Battersea, Brick Lane, Tower Hamlets, and the Docklands, highlighting daily life and the evolving urban environment. These images offer a unique glimpse into a decade defined by both hardship and innovation,

item.title: Trump's China Visit Viewed as Strategic Setback, Author Suggests Potential Side Deals
item.description: During President Donald Trump's state visit to China in May 2026, analysts and biographers have noted limited tangible gains for the United States. Author Michael Wolff, speaking on The Daily Beast's podcast 'Inside Trump's Head,' described the visit as a display of Chinese political, economic, and military dominance, framing it as a setback for Trump. Wolff emphasized that China has long been a central issue for Trump, dating back to his 2016 campaign and throughout his political career, but the nation's power has only grown over the past decade. According to Wolff, Trump often presents a posture of strength but tends to shift between confrontation and concession when his approach does not yield results. The visit appears to have been challenging for Trump personally, and Wolff predicts that the president may attempt to regain political and public perception by focusing on self-serving side deals, highlighting a transactional approach to foreign policy. The commentary suggests that ev

item.title: The Legacy of Linda Hayden in British Horror Films
item.description: Linda Hayden remains one of the most memorable figures of British cinema from the late 1960s and 1970s, recognized for her rare combination of youthful innocence and intense screen presence. She first gained attention as a teenager in the provocative drama 'Baby Love' (1969), which sparked both admiration and controversy. However, it was her role as the eerie Angel Blake in the 1971 folk-horror film 'The Blood on Satan’s Claw' that firmly established her as a cult figure. Hayden's performances were marked by a captivating versatility, allowing her to shift effortlessly between glamorous roles and dark, complex characters. Her piercing gaze and ethereal aura made her a standout presence in British horror, embodying the bold and experimental spirit of her era. Over the years, her work has continued to attract fans of vintage thrillers and classic horror, and she remains celebrated for her contributions to the genre. This retrospective showcases a selection of photographs capturing her un

item.title: Jack Ruby Shoots Lee Harvey Oswald During Dallas Police Transfer in 1963
item.description: On November 24, 1963, two days after the assassination of President John F. Kennedy, Lee Harvey Oswald was being transferred from the Dallas Police Headquarters to the county jail. During the transfer, nightclub owner Jack Ruby emerged from a crowd of reporters and police officers and shot Oswald at point-blank range with a .38-caliber revolver. Oswald succumbed to his injuries approximately two hours later at Parkland Memorial Hospital, the same hospital where President Kennedy had died. The shooting was broadcast live on national television, making it one of the earliest acts of real-time violence witnessed by a wide American audience. The moment was immortalized in photography, most notably by Robert H. Jackson of the Dallas Times Herald, whose image won the 1964 Pulitzer Prize for Photography. Additional photographs captured the precise moment Ruby fired, the aftermath including Ruby's hat on the floor, and his mugshot following the arrest. The incident remains one of the most shoc

item.title: The Complex Bond Between Jackie Kennedy Onassis and Lee Radziwill Through Photos
item.description: Jacqueline Kennedy Onassis and her younger sister Lee Radziwill shared a life marked by both deep affection and intense rivalry. Raised in New York’s upper-class society, Jackie was slightly older than Lee, and their childhood nicknames, “Jacks” and “Pekes,” reflected a close bond. Their mother, Janet Auchincloss, often encouraged competition between them. Jackie married John F. Kennedy in 1953, while Lee married Michael Canfield the same year and later Prince Stanislaw Albrecht Radziwill in 1959. While Jackie’s role evolved into that of First Lady after JFK’s election, Lee frequently accompanied her as confidante, even traveling with her to India and Pakistan. Despite their close public presence, Lee struggled with being overshadowed by her sister’s prominence. The sisters’ relationship further strained after Jackie married Aristotle Onassis in 1968, a man Lee had loved. Throughout the 1970s, Lee pursued her own career in interior design and acting, often relying on family support, wh

item.title: Cytospora Canker and Climate Stress Threaten Colorado Peach Orchards
item.description: In western Colorado, the Palisade peach, a regional staple, faces significant threats from cytospora canker, a fungal disease that affects woody trees globally. A recent survey found all orchards in Orchard Mesa infected, with some losing up to 50% of their fruit per tree. This disease, along with environmental stressors such as diminishing water supplies, spring frosts, variable winter temperatures, and unsuitable soil pH, has reduced the lifespan of peach trees in Colorado from 20 years to as little as 10 years. Cytospora canker typically enters through wounds caused by pruning, hail, or sudden freeze events, which are common in Colorado. Notably, the 2020 freeze led to tens of thousands of tree deaths and created ideal conditions for the fungus to spread. Management options include removing infected trees, protecting pruning wounds, and reducing tree stress, but these measures have limited success due to local climate challenges. Researchers from Colorado State University are develo

item.title: Children’s Creative Birdhouses in Cambodia Brought to Life from Their Drawings
item.description: In Siem Reap, Cambodia, designer Taekhan Yun conducted a unique workshop inviting ninety children to design birdhouses without any constraints or prior architectural knowledge. The project encouraged imagination over conventional design rules. Children first learned about local bird species and basic birdhouse geometry, then freely drew houses for birds according to their creativity. These crayon sketches were transformed into clay models and finally into fully constructed birdhouses using wood, cardboard, and paper, with the original crayon colors preserved and sealed with acrylic lacquer. The resulting birdhouses are both functional and sculptural, embodying the childlike creativity and spontaneity often lost in professional design. Installed at the children's school, these birdhouses serve as a record of imagination before self-doubt intervenes, blending participatory art, design education, and playful architecture.

item.title: Louisiana Senate Primary Highlights Trump-Backed Challenge to Senator Cassidy
item.description: The Louisiana Senate primary on Saturday is shaping up as a significant test for former President Donald Trump, who has endorsed Rep. Julia Letlow in an effort to unseat incumbent Republican Sen. Bill Cassidy. Cassidy, who voted to convict Trump during his second impeachment, faces strong opposition from both Letlow and Louisiana State Treasurer John Fleming, making a runoff likely if no candidate wins a majority. The race underscores Trump’s influence within the GOP and his ability to target fellow Republicans who opposed him. Cassidy has relied on his substantial financial advantage and support from Senate GOP leaders to campaign against Letlow, highlighting her background in higher education and diversity initiatives. Meanwhile, Letlow and Fleming have battled each other on the campaign trail, with Letlow’s team labeling Fleming a “Never Trumper” and scrutinizing his prior lobbying work. The primary is further complicated by a recently implemented closed primary system, which requir

item.title: Historical Photographs of Tennessee During the American Civil War
item.description: Tennessee played a crucial and turbulent role during the American Civil War, becoming the second most contested state after Virginia. Its strategic rivers, railroads, and geographic location earned it the title 'Gateway to the Deep South,' making it a key target for both Union and Confederate forces. Significant battles such as Shiloh, the 'Battle Above the Clouds' in Chattanooga, and the decisive clashes in Nashville shaped the course of the war. The state was deeply divided, with East Tennessee largely supporting the Union and West Tennessee leaning toward the Confederacy. This article presents rare and remarkable photographs documenting the Union Army's buildup, supply depots, and key battle sites, including Lookout Mountain, Chattanooga, and Nashville. Images also depict railroad yards, bridges, artillery parks, barracks, and local landscapes affected by the conflict. These photographs not only capture military activity but also provide insight into daily life and infrastructure du

item.title: The 1930 Cord L-29 Cabriolet: A Milestone in Automotive Design and Technology
item.description: The 1930 Cord L-29 Cabriolet stands as a landmark in automotive history, notable for being the first American production car to feature front-wheel drive. Designed by Al Leamy under the direction of E.L. Cord, the L-29 combined technical innovation with striking aesthetics. Its long, low profile, coffin-nosed radiator grille, and 125-inch wheelbase gave it a dramatic presence on the road. Under the hood, the Lycoming straight-eight engine delivered 125 horsepower, providing smooth and responsive performance that was exceptional for its size. The Cabriolet variant added a luxurious, convertible top and refined interior, blending technological advancement with the elegance of Art Deco design. This model exemplifies the ambition of early 20th-century American automotive engineering, showcasing a combination of functionality, style, and sophistication that set it apart from contemporaries. The car remains a symbol of innovation, elegance, and the forward-thinking spirit of the 1930s, highl

item.title: Supreme Court Weakens Voting Rights Act, Undermining Minority Representation
item.description: In a landmark 6-3 decision in Louisiana v. Callais, the conservative majority of the U.S. Supreme Court severely weakened Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act, ruling that protecting minority representation in congressional maps is unconstitutional. New York Times columnist Jamelle Bouie described the ruling as a profound betrayal of American democratic values. He emphasized that the Voting Rights Act of 1965 was a product of one of the most effective social movements in U.S. history, not an imposition by federal authorities, and revitalized democracy by ensuring voting rights for historically marginalized communities. Bouie also highlighted the irony of the current Supreme Court's decision, noting that most of its conservative justices were appointed by presidents who won the Electoral College but not the popular vote, contrasting their legitimacy with that of the Voting Rights Act, which was repeatedly reaffirmed by Congress for decades. The article underscores the historical importanc

item.title: Dental Calculus Reveals Mercury Use in Medieval Leprosy Treatment
item.description: A recent study led by Dr. Elena Fiorin and published in the Journal of Archaeological Science investigated the use of mercury-based treatments for leprosy in the late medieval period. Unlike previous research that focused on bones, this study analyzed dental calculus, or hardened plaque, from individuals buried in leprosaria cemeteries in England and France. Researchers found elevated mercury levels in these individuals, suggesting that mercury treatments were applied during their lifetime rather than being the result of environmental contamination. The study analyzed 76 individuals and 45 soil samples, showing that mercury was present in the bodies but not in surrounding soils, ruling out external sources. Variations in mercury levels among individuals may reflect clinical severity, access to treatment, or social status, as those buried in the leprosarium chapel exhibited higher levels, indicating privileged access to medical care. This research demonstrates that dental calculus is a

item.title: A Look Back at People and Their Simca Cars from the 1930s to 1960s
item.description: During the mid-20th century, Simca cars were more than just vehicles; they were symbols of freedom, prosperity, and personal style across Europe. From the compact Simca 5 to the sporty 1200S, these cars were cherished by families and individuals alike. The collection of vintage photographs showcases everyday life alongside these iconic French cars, capturing moments of joy, pride, and connection. The images span from the 1930s through the early 1960s and include scenes such as couples enjoying springtime drives, stylish ladies posing with their vehicles in city streets or countryside roads, and friends capturing the excitement of a weekend outing. Locations range from Paris and the French Riviera to Monaco and even Singapore, reflecting the international presence of Simca. Beyond the cars themselves, the photographs preserve fashion, leisure, and the social ambiance of the era, from formal attire and fur coats to casual countryside picnics. These snapshots serve as a visual history of

item.title: Historical Photographs of Thérèse of Lisieux Portraying Joan of Arc
item.description: Thérèse of Lisieux (1873–1897), also known as the Little Flower of Jesus, was a French Discalced Carmelite nun who is widely venerated today. Deeply inspired by the patriotic deeds of French heroines, especially Joan of Arc, Thérèse felt a strong desire to emulate them in her own spiritual life. She wrote plays and poems about Joan of Arc, drawing parallels between Joan's suffering in prison and her own struggles with illness. On January 21, 1894, she presented her first play to the Carmelite community, followed by a more ambitious production a year later. Between January 21 and March 25, 1895, Thérèse’s sister, Céline, photographed her five times dressed as Joan of Arc in the convent courtyard. Thérèse wore a brown wig over her Carmelite habit and a gold-paper costume. Tragically, these photographs were later misused by a con artist, Leo Taxil, who invented a fictional character, Diana Vaughan, to ridicule the Church. Taxil even used Thérèse's image in a public demonstration mocking r

item.title: Six Migrants Found Dead in Train Boxcar in Texas; Authorities Investigate Human Smuggling
item.description: Authorities in Laredo, Texas, discovered six deceased migrants inside a Union Pacific train boxcar on Sunday. The victims, comprising three men from Honduras and a woman and two men from Mexico, are believed to have died from heatstroke. Officials indicated that the train originated from Long Beach, California, and the migrants likely boarded the boxcar during a stop in Del Rio, Texas. The train continued eastward toward San Antonio and Laredo, where the bodies were found. Laredo Police Chief Miguel A. Rodriguez Jr. confirmed the involvement of federal authorities, including Homeland Security Investigations and the Texas Rangers, in what is being treated as a potential human smuggling case. Union Pacific employees assisted in the discovery and are cooperating with law enforcement. Local officials stressed the severe dangers of human trafficking and the inhumane conditions migrants face, urging individuals not to attempt such perilous journeys. Laredo Mayor Victor D. Treviño called the

item.title: The Life and Tragic Death of Ndugu M’Hali, Companion of Explorer Henry Morton Stanley
item.description: Ndugu M’Hali, also known as Kalulu, was an African boy born around 1865 in what is now Tanzania. Captured as a child during a slave raid, he was sold in Tabora and later acquired by the Victorian explorer Henry Morton Stanley in 1871. Stanley renamed him Kalulu, meaning “young antelope,” due to his agility and grace, and brought him to Europe and America. Kalulu became both a companion and a servant to Stanley, appearing in portraits and attending high-profile social events, even becoming the subject of Stanley’s 1873 book, which fictionalized his adventures. For a brief time, he attended school in London with the hope of one day acting as a colonial intermediary in Africa. In 1874, Kalulu joined Stanley on the arduous Trans-Africa Expedition to map the Congo River. Tragically, on March 28, 1877, Kalulu drowned at Livingstone Falls along with five crew members, prompting Stanley to name that section Kalulu Falls in his memory. Modern historians view his story critically, recognizing th

item.title: House Democrats Reveal DOJ Redacted Document Potentially Identifying Epstein Co-Conspirators
item.description: During a shadow hearing in Palm Beach, Florida, House Oversight Committee Democrats highlighted a key document that could reveal the identities of Jeffrey Epstein's co-conspirators. Representative Raja Krishnamoorthi (D-IL) emphasized that the Department of Justice (DOJ) had identified multiple individuals connected to Epstein's sex trafficking network in 2019, but the released document contained redactions for at least six people whose identities remain undisclosed. Epstein, who died in jail while awaiting trial on new sex trafficking charges, and Ghislaine Maxwell, who was convicted and is now reportedly seeking a pardon, are the only individuals prosecuted so far. Krishnamoorthi criticized what he called a 'corrupt administration of justice,' noting that other federal agencies under former President Trump hold evidence, including $1.5 billion in suspicious financial transactions that remain unproduced, which could shed light on the broader network. The hearing featured tearful testi

item.title: San Francisco Tech Workers Face Unequal Opportunities Amid AI Boom
item.description: The AI boom is creating an unprecedented wealth divide in the tech industry, especially in San Francisco. Menlo Ventures partner Deedy Das highlighted the growing gap in outcomes, noting that approximately 10,000 individuals—mostly founders and employees at companies like OpenAI, Anthropic, Nvidia, and others—have amassed retirement-level wealth exceeding $20 million. Meanwhile, the majority of tech workers, earning less than $500,000 annually, face uncertainty and limited prospects. Das also pointed out the emotional toll, with layoffs increasing and many software engineers questioning the relevance of their skills, leading to widespread anxiety about career paths and the future of work. The social media response has been mixed: some argue that these individuals are fortunate enough to simply choose happiness, while others note the ironic and harsh reality that the same AI technology serves as both a pathway to wealth and a disruptor of traditional careers. The situation underscores t

item.title: U.S. Cruise Passengers Begin 42-Day Hantavirus Quarantine in Nebraska
item.description: Following a deadly hantavirus outbreak on the MV Hondius cruise ship, more than a dozen American passengers have been placed in a 42-day quarantine at the University of Nebraska Medical Center's specialized facility. These passengers were potentially exposed to hantavirus but currently show no symptoms. During quarantine, they are monitored daily for signs of fatigue, fever, or other symptoms. Some may later transition to home quarantine under strict CDC guidance, which includes mask use, distancing, symptom checks, and avoiding shared items or crowded venues. Health officials emphasize that hantavirus is not easily transmitted, typically requiring prolonged contact with symptomatic individuals. Passengers are receiving care, food, and supplies in Nebraska, and local authorities are coordinating to ensure that those quarantined at home remain safe. Hantavirus can cause severe respiratory illness, which is why authorities are prioritizing both monitoring and access to advanced medical c

item.title: Record Low Temperature in Canada: −63°C Recorded in Snag, Yukon in 1947
item.description: On February 3, 1947, the small village of Snag in Yukon, Canada, experienced the coldest temperature ever officially recorded in the country and all of North America, reaching an astonishing −63°C (−81.4°F). To put this extreme cold into perspective, it is roughly equivalent to the average surface temperature of Mars. The temperature was so severe that the alcohol in thermometers fell below the measurable scale, requiring the weather observer, Gordon Toole, to manually mark the thermometer so it could be sent to Toronto for precise calibration. The air, dense and still due to the extreme cold, carried sounds over extraordinary distances; dogs barking from over 6 kilometers away could be clearly heard. The freezing conditions caused exhaled breath to solidify instantly, producing a hissing sound and forming fine white powder in the air. People walking outside left visible streaks of frozen breath that lingered for minutes. The Snag weather station operated from 1943 to 1966, and this re

item.title: Torrance York Explores Parkinson’s Through Photography
item.description: In this LensCulture interview, photographer Torrance York shares how her diagnosis with Parkinson’s disease became a catalyst for her artistic exploration. York’s project, Semaphore, uses photography to articulate the nuances of living with a movement disorder, blending everyday life, natural landscapes, and medical imagery to create a unique visual language. The images capture both the challenges and the moments of resilience and growth associated with her condition. York explains that photography provides her with a sense of control, slowing down her perception and allowing her to observe and interpret her surroundings thoughtfully. Semaphore also serves as a therapeutic tool, enriching her discussions with a psychologist trained in art therapy and fostering understanding of her own evolving experience. By combining precise compositions, recurring geometric forms, and metaphorical representations, the project reflects her goals, fears, and adaptive strategies, highlighting neuroplast

item.title: Implementing AI-Powered Grammar Checking and TeX Math Support in LibreOffice
item.description: Former Microsoft developer Keith Curtis shared a detailed account of his experience adding advanced features to LibreOffice, specifically an AI-powered grammar checker and support for TeX math. Drawing from his five years of experience working on Microsoft's RichEdit and Quill text components, Curtis explains the underlying data structures and algorithms that make text processing efficient, such as piece tables that allow operations on large documents without performance loss. The article emphasizes the challenges Curtis faced when adapting these concepts to LibreOffice, particularly dealing with UNO initialization variables in Python, handling sentence casing issues, duplicate words, and foreign-language syntax. His work culminated in a new LibreOffice extension, WriterAgent, which integrates generative AI editing into Writer, Calc, and Draw. Curtis also provides a link to the repository for users who wish to try the extension, promoting the idea of making LibreOffice and desktop appl

item.title: The Relationship Journey of Eric Clapton and Pattie Boyd in the 1970s
item.description: During the 1970s, Eric Clapton and Pattie Boyd's relationship evolved from unrequited love to a high-profile marriage that captured rock history. Initially, Clapton, deeply enamored with Boyd while she was married to George Harrison, expressed his feelings through a 1970 love letter and by sharing the iconic song 'Layla,' inspired by his obsession. Despite his advances, Boyd remained with Harrison at the time. As the decade progressed, both Clapton and Harrison faced marital challenges, drug abuse, and affairs. By 1974, Harrison and Boyd agreed to separate, leading to their divorce three years later. Around the same time, Clapton overcame heroin addiction, and he and Boyd began a romantic relationship with Harrison’s support. Clapton’s 1977 song 'Wonderful Tonight' celebrated Boyd, highlighting her presence in his life. The couple married on March 27, 1979, in a small ceremony in Tucson, Arizona, attended by only 40 guests, with Harrison giving his blessing and even humorously calling

item.title: Teenage Rob Lowe’s Early TV Debut in ABC Sitcom ‘A New Kind of Family’
item.description: Before becoming a household name and part of the iconic 'Brat Pack,' Rob Lowe made his television debut at the age of 15 in the short-lived ABC sitcom 'A New Kind of Family' (1979–1980). In the show, he portrayed Tony Flanagan, the teenage son of Kit Flanagan, played by Eileen Brennan. The series followed the story of two single mothers, one widowed and the other divorced, who accidentally lease the same house and decide to raise their families together to save money. Despite its unique premise and a promising young cast, including Lauri Hendler, David Hollander, and later additions like Telma Hopkins and a young Janet Jackson, the show struggled in the ratings. According to Lowe, it ranked last in the Nielsen ratings, 62 out of 62 shows at the time, leading to a mid-season retool and eventual cancellation in January 1980. Although the series was short-lived, it served as a stepping stone for Rob Lowe, who would go on to star in successful films like 'The Outsiders' (1983) and 'St. Elm

item.title: Jean-Louis Guégan and His Iconic Photography of 1960s-70s French Fashion
item.description: Jean-Louis Guégan was a celebrated French fashion photographer who left an indelible mark on the visual culture of the 1960s and 1970s. Renowned for his extensive work with the magazine L’Officiel de la Couture, Guégan documented the most iconic creations of the era, including Yves Saint Laurent’s pop-art inspired raincoats and Pierre Cardin’s futuristic 'Space Age' designs. Throughout his career, he collaborated with legendary fashion houses such as Chanel, Emanuel Ungaro, Lanvin, and Givenchy, capturing not only the clothes but also the essence of haute couture glamour. His lens also immortalized major stars of the time, including Sophia Loren and Brigitte Bardot. The photographs reveal meticulous attention to detail, from embroidered evening gowns and mink-trimmed sleeves to intricate jewelry and headpieces. Guégan’s work serves as a vital historical record, reflecting the evolution of French fashion and the interplay between clothing, accessories, and styling. Through his imagery,

item.title: The Cultural Significance of Women Smoking in Mid-20th Century Photography
item.description: In the mid-20th century, cigarettes became more than just a smoking habit—they were a symbol of style, sophistication, and social rebellion, particularly among women. Between the 1940s and 1960s, images of women with cigarettes were widely circulated in magazines, films, and advertisements, presenting a glamorous yet complex portrayal of femininity. Hollywood stars and urban trendsetters used cigarettes as a visual accessory to convey independence, poise, and modernity. The photos captured the allure of the 'femme fatale' and reflected a broader cultural shift in women’s roles and societal perception. Whether elegantly posed in ballrooms or casually enjoying a smoke in jazz clubs, these images highlighted a blend of elegance, defiance, and evolving female identity. Today, these vintage photographs offer not only nostalgia but also insight into the aesthetics, fashion, and social attitudes of a bygone era.

item.title: Myrna Dell and Her Enduring Legacy in Classic Hollywood
item.description: Myrna Dell (1924–2011) was a prominent actress during Hollywood's Golden Age, celebrated for her striking beauty, elegance, and versatility. She began her career as a showgirl in the Earl Carroll Revues, which provided her with the stage presence and charisma that later defined her screen performances. Throughout the 1940s and 1950s, Dell appeared in numerous films, often portraying the iconic 'femme fatale' in film noir and Westerns. Beyond her alluring on-screen roles, she earned recognition for her professionalism, poise, and the ability to leave a lasting impression in any scene. Her contributions to cinema continue to resonate with fans of classic films, serving as a timeless example of mid-century glamour. Today, vintage portraits of Dell allow viewers to experience the charm and elegance she embodied, offering a glimpse into an era where Hollywood style and grace were paramount.

item.title: Kurt Russell’s Transformative Portrayal of Elvis Presley in the 1979 TV Movie
item.description: Kurt Russell’s role as Elvis Presley in the 1979 TV movie 'Elvis' marked a pivotal moment in his acting career and began his long-standing collaboration with director John Carpenter. Airing on ABC and later released theatrically outside the U.S., the film demanded a detailed and immersive approach from Russell. To authentically capture Elvis, who had passed away just two years prior, Russell studied Presley’s speech, movements, and stage presence, while his singing was performed by Ronnie McDowell. Costume designer Miller Drake meticulously recreated Elvis’s iconic stage outfits, and Russell even used prosthetics and gained weight to portray different stages of Elvis’s life. Adding a unique layer to the production, Russell’s father, Bing, played Elvis’s father, Vernon Presley. Interestingly, Russell’s first film role as a child involved a comedic encounter with Elvis, foreshadowing this iconic portrayal. Despite a nearly three-hour runtime, the movie was filmed in just 30 days and achi

item.title: House Oversight Committee Examines Role of Epstein's Assistant in Abuse Operations
item.description: The U.S. House Oversight Committee is investigating Sarah Kellen, a former assistant to Jeffrey Epstein, for her alleged role in facilitating Epstein's criminal activities. Serving as his assistant from 2000, Kellen was described by Representative Suhas Subramanyam as a key figure who managed Epstein's schedule and operations, effectively acting as a gatekeeper. While some, including Committee Chairman James Comer, view Kellen as a potential victim within Epstein's network, Subramanyam suggested that evidence from the Epstein files indicates she may have acted as a co-conspirator. The committee has scheduled a transcribed interview with Kellen on May 21 to clarify her involvement and gather information about how Epstein was able to evade prosecution for his crimes for so long. The investigation seeks to understand the broader network that enabled Epstein's abuse and how those close to him may have contributed to or facilitated these activities. Lawmakers emphasize the importance of Kel

item.title: Ian McKellen Returns to Himself After Playing Falstaff
item.description: At 85 years old, renowned actor Ian McKellen undertook the iconic role of John Falstaff in a West End production of Shakespeare’s Henry IV, Parts I & II. To fully embody the character, McKellen allowed his hair and beard to grow for nearly a year. In June 2024, an on-stage accident forced him to leave the production mid-run, although the show continued for five more weeks without him. McKellen used this time to recover at home, waiting until the play concluded to fully step out of the Falstaff persona. On July 26, 2024, following 101 performances, he was photographed at home receiving a haircut that symbolized his return to his own identity. Photographer Frederic Aranda captured these intimate moments, documenting the physical and symbolic transformation from character back to actor. This series highlights the intersection of theater, personal ritual, and the subtle but profound ways an actor embodies and then relinquishes a role.

item.title: Former Intelligence Official Criticizes Trump’s Approach to Chinese Cybersecurity Threats
item.description: Sue Gordon, former deputy director of national intelligence, expressed serious concern over President Donald Trump's remarks regarding Chinese cyberattacks and intellectual property theft following a recent summit with Chinese leader Xi Jinping. Speaking with Nicolle Wallace on MS NOW, Gordon criticized Trump's statement that U.S. companies and Chinese companies have been engaging in similar behavior for decades, calling this notion incorrect. She emphasized that U.S. law maintains a clear distinction between government operations and private sector protections, while Chinese laws compel companies and citizens to provide information to authorities, creating an uneven playing field. Gordon highlighted the implications of this approach for the United States' relationships with allies and partners, suggesting that it risks undermining America’s global standing. She also stressed that although espionage is historically common, U.S. law creates boundaries that prevent the theft of intellect

item.title: US and Nigerian Forces Kill ISIS Senior Leader Abu-Bilal al-Minuki in Joint Operation
item.description: US and Nigerian forces conducted a joint operation that resulted in the death of Abu-Bilal al-Minuki, a senior ISIS leader, according to statements by US President Donald Trump and Nigerian officials. Trump described the mission as complex and meticulously planned, emphasizing that al-Minuki, who served as ISIS's second-in-command globally, would no longer be able to orchestrate attacks in Africa or against Americans. The operation took place near the Lake Chad Basin, with multiple militants reportedly killed alongside al-Minuki. US Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth highlighted months of surveillance and coordination with Nigerian forces, underscoring the strategic partnership in combating shared terrorist threats. Nigerian President Bola Ahmed Tinubu expressed gratitude for US collaboration and praised the operation as a demonstration of effective international security efforts. Al-Minuki had previously been mistakenly reported dead in 2024 during Nigerian military operations in northern

item.title: The Life and Legacy of Milunka Savić, Serbia’s Most Decorated Female Soldier
item.description: Milunka Savić, born in Serbia, made history by disguising herself as a man to fight in the Balkan Wars when her brother was too ill to serve. She joined the Serbian army under the name Milun Savić, and for nearly a year, she fought undetected, demonstrating exceptional skill as a soldier and grenadier. Her identity was revealed during the Second Balkan War when she was wounded at the Battle of Bregalnica. Despite military laws barring women from combat, she insisted on staying in the infantry and quickly earned recognition for her bravery. During World War I, Savić gained international fame as a member of the elite 'Iron Regiment.' She was particularly renowned for her precision with hand grenades and her ability to capture enemy soldiers single-handedly, earning multiple prestigious awards, including Serbia’s Order of the Star of Karađorđe, the French Croix de Guerre, the British Order of St Michael and St George, the Russian Order of St. George, and the French Legion of Honour. After

item.title: Trump and Xi Meet: Modest Advances in Trade and Diplomatic Relations
item.description: President Donald Trump concluded a two-day summit with Chinese leader Xi Jinping on May 15, 2026, in China, a meeting watched closely for signs of shifts in U.S.-China relations. Both leaders expressed positive tones, with Trump calling the trip 'incredible' and Xi describing it as the start of a 'new bilateral relationship.' Despite high expectations, analysts noted no major breakthroughs. Taiwan remained a central topic, with Xi reiterating its importance to China, while the U.S. maintained its longstanding policy opposing unilateral moves toward Taiwanese independence. Trump also mentioned arms sales to Taiwan, but no changes to U.S. commitments were made. On trade, progress was modest: agreements included limited sales of U.S. beef, 200 Boeing aircraft, and continued Nvidia chip purchases. Importantly, both nations agreed to establish a Board of Trade and Board of Investment to guide future economic engagement. Military communication was also highlighted, as Washington seeks a chan

item.title: Karl P. Schmidt’s Detailed Account of His Fatal Boomslang Snake Bite
item.description: In 1957, Karl P. Schmidt, a prominent herpetologist, suffered a fatal bite from a juvenile boomslang snake while handling it at the Chicago Natural History Museum. Believing that a young, rear-fanged snake could not deliver a lethal dose, Schmidt declined medical treatment, choosing instead to meticulously document the progression of symptoms from the venom. Over the course of a day, he recorded nausea, chills, bleeding from gums, fever, and hematuria, continuing his normal routines despite deteriorating health. His journal notes his breakfast the following morning, where he understated ongoing bleeding as “not excessive,” the last word he ever wrote. Schmidt died later that afternoon, with an autopsy revealing extensive internal bleeding caused by disseminated intravascular coagulation, a condition triggered by boomslang venom. Although antivenom exists, it was unavailable in Chicago in the 1950s. Schmidt’s death drew attention to the dangers of rear-fanged colubrid snakes and led to

item.title: German Chancellor Merz Advises Against His Children Moving to the U.S. Amid Social Concerns
item.description: German Chancellor Friedrich Merz recently expressed concerns about the current social and economic climate in the United States, telling an audience of young Catholics that he would not recommend his children study or work in the country at this time. Merz cited a 'social climate' that has 'suddenly developed,' noting that even highly educated Americans are facing employment difficulties. Former Republican strategist Steve Schmidt interpreted Merz's comments as a serious warning rather than mere political criticism, emphasizing the significance of such a statement from a conservative Atlanticist leader who has long admired the U.S. Schmidt suggested that Merz's perspective is informed by Germany's historical confrontation with fascism, allowing him to recognize warning signs that Americans may overlook. The discussion also highlighted ongoing tensions between Merz and former President Donald Trump, particularly regarding U.S. foreign policy and perceived mismanagement on the internatio

item.title: David Masoko Explores Intimate and Ethical Street Photography
item.description: David Masoko’s series “Dislocated Presences” offers a fresh perspective on street photography by focusing on subtle gestures and transitional moments rather than traditional decisive scenes. Working in cities like London, Bangkok, Hong Kong, and Tokyo, Masoko captures fragments of everyday life—partial gestures, obscured faces, and fleeting interactions. His work emphasizes the space between people and the ethical considerations of photographing strangers, questioning the balance between visibility and privacy in public spaces. Influenced by thinkers like Walter Benjamin and Martin Heidegger, Masoko treats public life as overlapping solitudes, avoiding spectacle and focusing on quiet, reflective moments. During the pandemic, this focus on distance and invisibility intensified, shaping his interest in what remains outside the frame. His images, often presented as diptychs with reduced color and softened focus, invite viewers to interpret the gaps between frames as actively as the conten

item.title: Trump Administration Removes Hundreds of Diplomats During Ongoing Global Crises
item.description: The Trump administration has recently forced out hundreds of U.S. diplomats, raising concerns about the country’s capacity to respond to ongoing international crises. According to reports, nearly 250 foreign service officers received emails on Friday notifying them that their positions had been terminated immediately. This reduction in force affects staff who could have provided crucial guidance on critical conflicts, such as the war in Iran. Former diplomats have noted that the situation is worsened by the voluntary departure of many seasoned personnel, with around 2,000 officers leaving the State Department last year alone. Additionally, more than 100 diplomatic posts in sensitive regions, including the Middle East, Ukraine, and Russia, remain without Senate-confirmed ambassadors. Critics highlight that key diplomatic negotiations are increasingly being managed by business associates and family members of President Trump, rather than experienced foreign service professionals. This un

item.title: Nectar Social Secures $30 Million Series A to Expand AI Marketing Platform
item.description: Nectar Social, an AI-powered marketing operating system, announced a $30 million Series A funding round led by Menlo Ventures and its Anthology Fund, affiliated with Anthropic. Founded by former Meta employees Misbah and Farah Uraizee, the company exited stealth mode last year and offers autonomous AI agents that help brands manage social activity, moderation, creator workflows, competitive intelligence, and commerce conversations in a single platform. Nectar Social also leverages data partnerships with platforms like Meta and Reddit, allowing brands to consolidate insights across multiple social channels. The funding will support hiring in applied AI, engineering, and go-to-market teams, aiming to enhance its platform’s ability to let brands maintain a consistent presence across all social channels. Notable clients include Liquid Death, Figma, and e.l.f. Beauty. Other investors participating in the round include Gwyneth Paltrow’s Kinship Ventures, GV, and True Ventures. CEO Misbah Ura

item.title: Buster Keaton Tested His Voice for the Transition to Talking Films
item.description: In the late 1920s, silent film legend Buster Keaton undertook an unusual yet practical task: he listened to recordings of his own voice to ensure it matched the persona that had made him famous, the stoic 'Great Stone Face.' The transition from silent films to 'talkies' posed a significant challenge for many actors, as audiences expected a voice that complemented their established screen image. Keaton, fascinated by both performance and the mechanical aspects of filmmaking, carefully evaluated how his deep, slightly Midwestern, gravelly voice would sound on early sound equipment like Vitaphone, which was prone to distorting pitch and tone. Unlike some of his contemporaries who struggled to adapt, Keaton’s voice proved well-suited to the silent-film persona he had developed over the previous decade. His first full-length talking picture, 'Free and Easy' (1930), confirmed that his natural speaking style translated effectively to sound film. While his career later faced setbacks due to st

item.title: Exploring the Legacy of the 1949 Jaguar XK120 Alloy Roadster
item.description: The 1949 Jaguar XK120 Alloy Roadster stands as an iconic symbol of automotive innovation and post-war design elegance. Debuting at the London Motor Show, its sleek and aerodynamic curves immediately captured public attention, reshaping expectations for sports cars of the era. The '120' in its name referenced its top speed of 120 mph, making it the fastest production car in the world at the time. Each early model was handcrafted with an aluminum body over a traditional ash wood frame, a combination that offered both strength and a distinctive artisanal touch. Its long bonnet, graceful lines, and elegant silhouette made the XK120 not only a performance vehicle but also a work of art that embodied freedom and adventure on the open road. Between 1949 and 1954, over 12,000 units were produced, with more than 85 percent exported internationally. Today, the XK120 is celebrated for its timeless design, engineering excellence, and historical significance in the evolution of sports cars.

item.title: Camille Seaman on Photographing Supercell Storms and Icebergs
item.description: In this in-depth interview, photographer Camille Seaman discusses her acclaimed series featured in the Prix Pictet 'Storm' cycle. Best known for her 'Last Iceberg' project capturing Arctic and Antarctic ice formations, Seaman has more recently focused on the dramatic power of supercell thunderstorms in the American Great Plains. Her work explores themes of duality—beauty and destruction, creation and transience—while emphasizing humanity's deep connection to nature. Seaman shares her personal journey from a challenging childhood in New York City, where photography became an outlet during periods of homelessness, to finding her voice as an artist. Influenced by mentors like Steve McCurry, she learned to master light and composition. Her path to polar regions began unexpectedly and evolved into a mission to document fleeting natural phenomena like icebergs and storms, which she sees as metaphors for our own impermanence. Seaman addresses climate change impacts, noting increased tornado a

item.title: Study Links Low Brain Choline Levels to Anxiety Disorders
item.description: Researchers at the University of California - Davis Health have identified a notable chemical difference in the brains of people with anxiety disorders. A comprehensive analysis of 25 prior studies involving 712 participants revealed that individuals with anxiety consistently showed about 8% lower levels of choline, a nutrient essential for brain function, compared to those without anxiety. The most pronounced differences appeared in the prefrontal cortex, the area responsible for emotional regulation, decision-making, and behavior control. Choline supports cell structure, memory, mood regulation, and nerve signaling, and although the body can produce a small amount, most of it must come from diet. Anxiety disorders, which affect roughly 30% of U.S. adults, are linked to heightened stress responses and overactivity in brain regions such as the amygdala and prefrontal cortex. While the findings suggest a possible nutritional angle for managing anxiety, researchers emphasize that increas

item.title: Democrats warn GOP redistricting may disproportionately impact Black lawmakers in South Carolina
item.description: Following a Supreme Court ruling that weakened a key provision of the Voting Rights Act, Republican-led states have accelerated efforts to redraw congressional district maps, a move widely seen as politically strategic ahead of the November elections. Democratic strategists, including advisers to Rep. Jim Clyburn (D-SC) and Rep. Shomari Figures (D-AL), have voiced concerns that these efforts disproportionately target Black lawmakers and could roll back decades of political representation for Black communities. South Carolina, where Clyburn is the sole Black Democratic representative, has become a focal point, with warnings that the redistricting push could backfire politically. Meanwhile, other news items covered by the same outlet highlight rising tensions internationally, including criticism from German Chancellor Friedrich Merz regarding U.S. social and economic conditions, as well as reports of selective targeting of female journalists by FBI Director Kash Patel. Additionally, econ

item.title: Rep. Don Beyer Challenges Eric Trump Claim About Family Investments With Financial Disclosure
item.description: Rep. Don Beyer (D-Va.) publicly challenged Eric Trump's claim that President Donald Trump's assets are held in a blind trust that does not involve individual stock transactions. Beyer shared a recently released financial disclosure showing that Trump personally bought and sold shares of Nvidia, including transactions totaling millions of dollars, just days before Nvidia received U.S. approval to sell advanced chips to China. The disclosure, signed by Trump on May 8, 2026, documents thousands of stock purchases and sales in the first quarter of the year. This revelation sparked criticism from Sen. Elizabeth Warren and others, highlighting potential conflicts of interest, especially concerning U.S. national security. Eric Trump had asserted that all of his father's assets were in a blind trust and denied any individual stock trading by the family. The issue adds to ongoing scrutiny over Trump's financial activities, as previous ethics officials have questioned the effectiveness of his so

item.title: Ursula Andress in the 1970s: Her Transition to European Cinema and Enduring Icon Status
item.description: Ursula Andress, the Swiss actress and model born on March 19, 1936, became an international sex symbol after her iconic role as Honey Ryder in the 1962 James Bond film Dr. No. The 1970s marked a significant evolution in her career as she shifted from Hollywood prominence to a prolific presence in European, particularly Italian, cinema. During this decade, Andress starred in a variety of Italian genre films, including commedia sexy all'italiana and poliziotteschi crime dramas. One notable project was the 1971 Western Red Sun, which brought together an impressive international cast featuring Charles Bronson, Alain Delon, and Toshirō Mifune. By the mid-1970s, she had become one of Italy's highest-paid actresses, with commercial successes such as The Sensuous Nurse (L'infermiera). In 1978, she took on a more intense role in the controversial cannibal film The Mountain of the Cannibal God. Throughout the decade, Andress maintained her status as a fashion and beauty icon with her athletic, A

item.title: Glenn Close’s Transformative Role as Marquise de Merteuil in Dangerous Liaisons
item.description: On the set of the 1988 film Dangerous Liaisons, Glenn Close underwent an intense transformation to portray Marquise Isabelle de Merteuil. Filmed in authentic French châteaus, the production prioritized historical accuracy, requiring Close to wear period-accurate corsets and undergarments that restricted her waist to 24.5 inches. She described her costumes as armor and noted that the physical constraints helped her embody the character's controlled, predatory presence. Close arrived in Paris with her seven-week-old daughter, describing her post-pregnancy body humorously as 'looking like a loaf of bread.' Despite the physical challenges—including difficulty breathing and eating during long shoot days—she credited the experience with enhancing her performance. Close’s dedication extended to conceptualizing the film's final shot to convey the Marquise's inner turmoil, and she maintained a commanding on-set presence. Her chemistry with co-star John Malkovich was built on theater experience,

item.title: Milan Design Week 2026 Features Striking Inflatable Installations
item.description: Milan Design Week 2026 stood out for its creative use of inflatable structures and air-filled installations that transformed public and historic spaces across the city. The event embraced bold, playful designs that turned the urban landscape into an interactive playground. Among the highlights was Moncler’s giant maroon octopus installation at 10 Corso Como, which enveloped the building’s facade with tentacles reaching through windows, creating a dramatic visual impact from the street and a whimsical dining experience inside. Another notable piece was Sara Ricciardi Studio’s 'Serotonin: The Chemistry of Happiness' for American Express at the Pinacoteca di Brera. This installation featured large tie-dyed balloon spheres in blue and pink that appeared to breathe and move, visualizing the concept of joy within the historic stone arches. Škoda’s collaboration with Ulises Studio at Palazzo Senato converted the historic palace into an immersive video game-like environment complete with slide

item.title: Celebrating Kathleen Turner’s Iconic Career in 1980s Film
item.description: Kathleen Turner emerged as one of the most influential actresses of the 1980s, captivating audiences with her unique blend of sultry charm, intelligence, and commanding presence. Known for her husky voice and magnetic screen aura, Turner redefined the archetype of the modern 'femme fatale' in the neo-noir thriller Body Heat (1981). Her versatility allowed her to shine in a wide range of genres—from the adventurous romance of Romancing the Stone to the dark comedy of The War of the Roses. Beyond her striking looks, Turner’s bold energy and ability to balance drama and humor established her as a true cinematic powerhouse. Her influence extended beyond acting; she also provided the (originally uncredited) voice for Jessica Rabbit in Who Framed Roger Rabbit, further showcasing her talent. Today, Turner is celebrated as a cultural icon whose work left a lasting mark on 1980s cinema. Vintage photographs of her career highlight both her elegance and her unapologetic charisma, reminding audien

item.title: Salon Columnist Accuses FBI Director Kash Patel of Selectively Investigating Female Journalists
item.description: A Salon columnist has highlighted what she describes as a pattern of FBI investigations targeting female journalists who have published critical stories about FBI Director Kash Patel. According to the report, three women journalists—Hannah Natanson of The Washington Post, Elizabeth Williamson of The New York Times, and Sarah Fitzpatrick of The Atlantic—have faced actions including a pre-dawn home raid, potential stalking charge probes, and criminal leak investigations. These actions relate to their reporting on federal workforce reductions, Patel's alleged use of FBI resources for personal matters, and claims about his management style and personal conduct. In contrast, male journalists from outlets like The Wall Street Journal have published similar critical pieces without reported federal retaliation. The columnist argues this selective enforcement constitutes gender-based intimidation aimed at creating a chilling effect on accountability journalism. No prosecutions of journalists ha

item.title: NYT Reporter Defends Press Freedom After Trump's Treason Accusation
item.description: New York Times reporter David Sanger responded to President Donald Trump's public criticism after being labeled 'treasonous' for his reporting on U.S. military actions in Iran. During a confrontation on Air Force One, Sanger pressed Trump on the continuation of the Iran attack despite the lack of desired political changes. In response, Trump accused Sanger and major news organizations, including The New York Times and CNN, of reporting false information and suggested that their coverage was treasonous. Sanger countered by emphasizing that responsible journalism is not treason and highlighted that the attempt to intimidate the press undermines the First Amendment. CNN journalist Jake Tapper also condemned Trump's statements, calling them potentially dangerous and deranged. The New York Times issued a statement reinforcing Sanger's points, stating that reporting facts—even when they challenge government claims—is a cornerstone of a free press. The article underscores ongoing tensions bet

item.title: Behind-the-Scenes Portraits of Shirley MacLaine Filming Irma la Douce in 1963
item.description: These evocative behind-the-scenes portraits capture actress Shirley MacLaine during the production of the 1963 classic film Irma la Douce, directed by Billy Wilder. MacLaine stars as Irma, a spirited Parisian prostitute, in this colorful comedy that also features Jack Lemmon. The images showcase her vibrant performance, often highlighted by her character's signature green stockings and playful energy. Photographed on set, the portraits reveal MacLaine transitioning seamlessly between moments of sparkling laughter and focused concentration, offering insight into her dedicated acting process. The whimsical Technicolor backdrop of a recreated Paris enhances the timeless appeal of these shots, emphasizing both her natural beauty and intuitive depth as a performer. Irma la Douce remains a celebrated masterpiece from the 1960s, known for its blend of humor, romance, and elaborate production design. These rare photographs celebrate MacLaine's charisma and the film's enduring legacy in cinema

item.title: Suspected Drunk Driver to Face Charges After Reckless Crash Injures Motorcyclist
item.description: Authorities in Klang, Malaysia, are set to formally charge a man suspected of driving under the influence after a serious traffic incident left a motorcyclist critically injured. The suspect, a man in his 30s, allegedly drove recklessly against traffic for approximately 3.5 kilometers from Port Klang toward Shah Alam before colliding with the motorcyclist early Wednesday morning along Persiaran Kota. Police investigations confirmed that the driver tested positive for alcohol during initial screenings. The case is being pursued under Section 44(1A) of the Road Transport Act 1987, which addresses causing injury while driving under the influence of alcohol or drugs and carries a prison sentence of seven to ten years, along with a fine ranging from RM30,000 to RM50,000. A short video clip of the incident, showing the suspect's vehicle traveling against traffic, went viral on social media, drawing public attention to the dangers of reckless driving under the influence. The suspect remains i

item.title: 84-Year-Old Man Missing in Tampines Found Safe by Singapore Police
item.description: Singapore police announced on May 16, 2026, that an 84-year-old man reported missing the previous day has been located and is safe. Mr. U Swee, also known as Chang Yann Ching, was last seen around 2 p.m. on May 15 wearing a blue checkered shirt and black shorts near Block 961C Tampines Street 96. The authorities had issued a public appeal for information earlier that day regarding his whereabouts. This resolution comes as a relief for his family and the community. The case marks the third missing person announcement by police on May 16. Earlier appeals were made for two missing female teenagers, with one of them also confirmed found later in the day. Police encourage anyone with information on missing persons to contact the hotline at 1800-255-0000 or submit details via the online portal at www.police.gov.sg/i-witness, assuring strict confidentiality. This incident highlights the efficiency of Singapore's public safety systems in quickly addressing and resolving cases involving vulnera

item.title: Trump's Key Foreign Policy Goals Face Setbacks Amid Economic and Geopolitical Challenges
item.description: U.S. President Donald Trump's second term is facing a series of setbacks, with his ambitious foreign policy goals encountering major obstacles. His recent trip to China concluded without any substantial trade agreements, and President Xi Jinping emphasized Taiwan's strategic importance over U.S. investment interests. Analysts from the Washington Post highlight that this comes after a year marked by declining approval ratings, rising inflation, a continuing conflict in Iran, and energy price volatility. Many of Trump's earlier foreign policy initiatives, including efforts in Ukraine and Iran, have either stalled or shifted toward military engagement. The impact on business confidence was evident as Boeing's stock dropped 8 percent during his visit. While Trump has claimed success in facilitating meetings between U.S. executives and Chinese officials, tangible outcomes from these discussions remain limited. Overall, experts suggest that the president now navigates international diplomacy

item.title: Astronomers Capture the Clearest Image of a Cosmic Web Filament Connecting Galaxies
item.description: Astronomers have captured the sharpest direct image ever of a filament in the cosmic web, a massive network of matter that connects galaxies across the Universe. This glowing strand spans roughly 3 million light-years and links two galaxies that existed nearly 12 billion years ago, providing a rare glimpse into the early Universe. Using the MUSE instrument on the Very Large Telescope in Chile, researchers conducted hundreds of hours of observations to detect the faint intergalactic gas, which is normally difficult to observe due to its low brightness. By studying this filament, scientists can better understand how galaxies receive gas, which fuels star formation and galaxy growth. The observations also align well with supercomputer simulations, confirming current theories about the distribution of matter in the cosmos. The study, led by Davide Tornotti from the University of Milano-Bicocca and the Max Planck Institute for Astrophysics, marks a significant milestone in mapping the Unive

item.title: John Lennon and Yoko Ono’s Historic Wedding in Gibraltar
item.description: On March 20, 1969, John Lennon and Yoko Ono were married in Gibraltar, marking one of the most unconventional and iconic weddings in pop culture history. Initially, the couple wanted to marry on a cross-channel ferry to France or in Paris itself, but legal requirements in both countries made this impossible. With the help of Apple employee Peter Brown, they arranged a quick ceremony at the British Consulate Office in Gibraltar. The wedding lasted only ten minutes, with Ono wearing a white miniskirt, knee socks, and a floppy hat, while Lennon donned a white suit and turtleneck jumper. Lennon described the location as symbolically meaningful, calling it the 'Gateway to the World.' After the ceremony, the couple returned to their Paris hotel and began a unique honeymoon that included a week-long peace campaign in Amsterdam, famously known as a 'bed-in,' and another in Montreal where they recorded the protest anthem 'Give Peace a Chance.' Their wedding and honeymoon were immortalized in Le

item.title: George Rodriguez’s 1978 Portraits Capture Young Janet Jackson’s Early Career
item.description: In 1978, legendary Chicano photographer George Rodriguez captured intimate portraits of a 12-year-old Janet Jackson, providing a rare glimpse into the early life of the future pop superstar. At this time, Janet was establishing her career independently from her famous siblings, including Michael and La Toya Jackson. She had already made her television debut at the age of 10 on a CBS variety show, performing musical numbers, dancing, and participating in comedy sketches. Her acting talent became even more evident when producer Norman Lear cast her as Penny Gordon Woods on the sitcom 'Good Times' (1977–1979), a role that tackled sensitive issues of child abuse and demonstrated her emotional depth. Following this, Janet starred as Jojo Ashton in the ABC sitcom 'A New Kind of Family' (1979), solidifying her presence on network television. These photographs by Rodriguez not only document a critical transitional phase in Janet’s career but also reflect the cultural and artistic environment o

item.title: How Motivational Quotes Helped Me Heal After Divorce
item.description: After nearly three decades of marriage, Stephanie Vuckovic found herself devastated when her husband left, stating they were 'better off as friends.' Struggling with grief and loss, she turned to social media and discovered motivational quotes that emphasized self-worth, healing, and resilience. These brief but powerful messages became a crucial support system, supplementing the encouragement from friends, family, and therapy. Over time, the daily reminders helped her regain confidence, manage emotional pain, and navigate life independently while still being present for her adult children. Although she acknowledges that motivational content can sometimes be seen as simplistic or superficial, Stephanie credits these quotes with amplifying her healing process during the most challenging months. Eventually, she reached a point where she no longer needed the constant daily reinforcement, having rebuilt her life, focused on financial stability, reconnected with friends, and even embraced la

item.title: Alfred Stevens: Belgian Master Painter of Elegant 19th-Century Parisian Women
item.description: Alfred Stevens (1823–1906) was a prominent Belgian painter celebrated for his detailed portrayals of high-society women in Paris during the Second Empire period. Known for his exceptional technical skill, Stevens specialized in intimate domestic scenes featuring elegant ladies in luxurious interiors. His works stand out for their masterful rendering of textures, including the sheen of silk fabrics, delicate lace, and intricate Oriental carpets. Influenced by Dutch Masters and the Japonisme trend, his paintings go beyond mere depictions of fashion to explore mood, atmosphere, and everyday refined life. Stevens maintained close friendships with key figures like Édouard Manet and Edgar Degas, bridging traditional Academic art with emerging Impressionist techniques. The article showcases numerous examples of his oeuvre, such as 'A Girl Reading,' 'An Elegant Lady Indoors,' 'The Japanese Parisienne,' 'Lady with a Fan,' and 'The Bath.' These pieces capture quiet moments of contemplation, leis

item.title: Potential Motor Oil Shortages in the U.S. Amid Tensions with Iran
item.description: Reports indicate that the United States could soon face shortages of motor oil due to ongoing military tensions with Iran. The situation has drawn comparisons to the toilet paper shortages seen during the COVID-19 pandemic, reflecting public concern over supply chain disruptions. Several outlets, including Yahoo Autos and Axios, have highlighted how the conflict may affect the availability of lubricating fluids essential for vehicles. Alleged internal memos from companies like AutoZone and Toyota service departments suggest preparations for rationing motor oil stocks, though their authenticity has not been verified. Experts, such as Amanda Hay from the Independent Lubricant Manufacturers Association, warn that actual shortages are beginning to appear, signaling a possible wider impact on the automotive sector. Commentary on social media underscores the anxiety around the situation, with some placing blame on President Donald Trump's policies for creating economic strain. While the full

item.title: Inside Peridot Bar: A Green Gem-Inspired Lounge in Hong Kong
item.description: Perched on the 38th floor of The Henderson in Hong Kong, Peridot Bar is a visually striking cocktail lounge designed by Studio Paolo Ferrari. Wrapped almost entirely in soft emerald plaster, the interior references the yellowish-green gemstone peridot. The space prioritizes an immersive, tranquil experience over typical skyline views, featuring fluid, continuous surfaces where walls, ceiling, and floor blend seamlessly. Frosted acrylic lights form a grid that casts a gentle pixel-like glow, while mirrors reflect fragments of the city inside, adding depth. Anchoring the design is a massive green marble bar, complemented by custom lacquered furniture, plush mohair seating, stainless steel accents, and marble details. The combination of color, material, and form mirrors the sculptural elegance of Zaha Hadid's building exterior, creating a futuristic yet welcoming atmosphere. Rather than a conventional nightlife venue, Peridot Bar feels like stepping into a luminous gemstone, offering both

item.title: Rep. Mike Haridopolos Faces Direct Questions from Voters on C-SPAN About Trump and Economic Concerns
item.description: During a live C-SPAN broadcast, Republican Rep. Mike Haridopolos of Florida was confronted by frustrated voters raising concerns about President Donald Trump's policies and statements. One caller, an independent voter from Georgia, challenged Haridopolos on why Republicans continue to echo Trump's commentary on inflation and gas prices, questioning the factual basis of the party's messaging. Haridopolos responded by defending U.S. foreign policy, particularly regarding Iran's nuclear capabilities, while acknowledging differing opinions. Another caller from New York criticized Trump's international image, mentioning a personal experience in Europe and questioning America's respect abroad. Haridopolos admitted rising gas prices were a challenge but pointed to increased oil production in the U.S. and Venezuela as potential mitigations. The exchanges highlight the tension between elected officials and constituents over economic concerns, global diplomacy, and public perception of leadershi

item.title: CBS News Prepares Changes to '60 Minutes' Under Bari Weiss' Leadership
item.description: CBS News is facing internal uncertainty as editor-in-chief Bari Weiss plans to implement significant changes to the iconic news program '60 Minutes' in the upcoming season. Sources indicate that while the format and investigative focus of the show will remain, Weiss is expected to imprint her editorial vision, which has caused concern among staff and industry observers. Layoffs are anticipated, and insiders describe an atmosphere of apprehension, fearing major disruptions. High-profile correspondents Lesley Stahl and Scott Pelley are expected to stay with the program, though editorial decisions, such as recent assignments, have raised questions about Weiss' influence and hierarchy. External journalists, including CNN's Christiane Amanpour, have expressed concern over the potential ideological realignment of CBS News under the ownership of Skydance Media and Paramount Global. Despite these changes, '60 Minutes' continues to hold a reputation as a top-rated, influential news magazine wit

item.title: Federal Judge Condemns ICE for Targeted Arrest and Publicizing It Online
item.description: A federal judge has strongly criticized U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) for orchestrating a highly publicized arrest of Christian Cerna, a 28-year-old carpenter from Boyle Heights, following his participation in a protest against aggressive federal immigration policies in Los Angeles. On June 11, 2025, Cerna was driving with his partner and two children when ICE agents rammed his vehicle and approached with weapons and flash-bangs, while another agent filmed the incident. Despite Cerna surrendering immediately, DHS shared high-resolution footage online labeling him a 'violent rioter' and falsely claiming he tried to flee. Cerna was initially charged with felony assault, potentially carrying an eight-year sentence, but was released after a week and placed under house arrest with GPS monitoring. The incident caused significant distress to Cerna and his family, including stress-induced medical issues and severe anxiety in his children. Cerna later pled guilty to a misdemean

item.title: Amazon ends support for older Kindles, prompting users to modify devices
item.description: Amazon announced that it will end technical support for several older Kindle models on May 20, 2026. Affected devices include early generations of Kindle e-readers and Kindle Fire tablets, such as the 1st and 2nd Generation Kindle, Kindle DX, Kindle Keyboard, and early Kindle Fire models. After this date, these devices will only be able to access content already downloaded, and users will no longer receive software updates or support. As a result, many Kindle owners are turning to jailbreaking as a way to extend the functionality of their devices.
